title: Java Generics Wildcard Copy
description: Practice reading from an extends wildcard and writing into a concrete collection.
learningGoals: Use an upper-bounded wildcard when a method only reads values from a source list.
Keep writes directed at a concrete destination type so the compiler can prove safety.

completionChecks.prompt: Why does target.addAll(source) compile in copyScores?
choices: addAll accepts elements from a Collection whose element type extends Number
List<Integer> is a direct subtype of List<Number>
The wildcard removes generic type checks at compile time
The method converts every Integer into Double before adding it
completionChecks.explanation: List<Number>.addAll can accept a source collection whose elements are Number or a subtype of Number, so List<Integer> can be copied into it.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens if copyScores tries source.add(1) inside the method?
choices: It appends 1 to every caller's source list
It compiles only when source is backed by ArrayList
It fails to compile because ? extends Number is not a safe write target
It throws ClassCastException after the method returns
completionChecks.explanation: A List<? extends Number> may be a List<Integer>, List<Double>, or another subtype list, so the compiler cannot allow adding an arbitrary Number.

completionChecks.prompt: What happens after copyScores(rawScores, reportScores); reportScores.add(10.5); runs?
choices: rawScores also contains 10.5 because addAll links the lists
The call throws ClassCastException when the Integer values are copied
The code fails to compile because List<Integer> cannot be passed to the method
completionChecks.explanation: The trap is mixing invariance with bounded reads; the source can be read as Numbers, while the concrete List<Number> target can accept Double.
